item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean… the story plays well as a cautionary tale against rash destruction actions against the anomalies but… why the hell are the containment procedures feeding it one d-class every so often? Simply closing the roads seems like it could work, but apparently there's some need for carnage that the Foundation needs to fullfill. Still, IMO it deserves a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, it aged pretty bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The tone issues, basis in an overdone trope (phantom hitchhiker) as well as the idiotic actions of the head researcher followed by the Foundation deciding to keep the dumb sod employed instead of D- classing him right away and forcing him to do the parents job all add up to a -1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think, Doc L. should have been demoted to D-Class personel in "recognition of his brilliance".

Junior sounds too nice for him.

But, cool story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much obliged x2

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've got you covered.

[[module CSS]]
@import url(/component:scp-pride/code/1);
[[/module]]

Just stick this at the top of one of your articles and you should have the SCP pride logo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see, much obliged.

This is a bit off topic, but could you (or anyone really) point me towards how to make that logo choice when posting?
I wasn't active when the variant debuted so missed any discussion of how to use it, and my coding skill is strictly copy-paste limited.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nope, Bright just added it a month ago. There's tons of articles that use the SCP pride logo, just up to the author really.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why does this page have the rainbow logo?
Is it just because this was first posted during pride and the variant sticks?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The most elite scp of all time

item.title: image source
item.content:encoded:
Photo of the girl is taken from the Outside Lands project, which apparently is chronicling the history of San Francisco?

http://www.outsidelands.org/Display//images/weiss-lincoln-1950s.jpg

It's a high school girl in the 1950s, sitting outside the auditorium for Lincoln High School as it was under construction. Copyright unknown, so we can't assume it's properly licensed. http://www.outsidelands.org/Display//images/weiss-lincoln-1950s.jpg

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Super geeked Night mind referenced this one in his SCP Vault 2 video! I'm a nerd, I know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've always wanted to see the back-from-the-decom SCPs.
And now I question myself how did I not find this before.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wouldn't have noticed if not for this comment. That said, I think it still doesn't go down well for me, but at least it makes sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A necro, but I like that little change that seems to have been added in after I last read this one. For me it does give a legitimate reason for the Foundation not to get rid of him and fixes the issue referred to in the comments, though I don't know if a lot of people noticed.

I like to think of the discussion on his punishment going sort of like this:

O5-1: Shoot the idiot.
O5-2: Shoot the idiot.
O5-3: Wait, I have a better idea…
O5-1: Ooo…
O5-2: I like this.

And I can probably imagine them getting some satisfaction on hearing about the doctor's final fate…at least until they learned that the SCP became active again.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: I like it.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Links to 2539 and 1401 added to current proposal.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: I feel like a link to SCP-2539 would be amazing in this article. I do enjoy the current link as well.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I feel like there's a few more "haunted highway" type articles. Finding them is going to be challenging because there doesn't seem to be a tag for ghosts (which maybe there should be?).

ETA: SCP-1401 is another haunted highway skip.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at anytime.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Between SCP-1337 and SCP-973, the Foundation sure has a nasty track record for pissing off ghosts and making them more deadly than they were before. Given this track record, you'd think there would be an internal document noting such occurances. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

SCP-1337's range of movement has extended to all of the back roads of Muncie. If a vehicle stops for SCP-1337, it vanishes from view, to reappear on another road. Should a vehicle pass SCP-1337 by, it will then appear inside the vehicle, where it will re-enact the methods of its murder upon the driver of the vehicle. SCP-1337 will only appear for vehicles containing one person.

To this:

SCP-1337's range of movement has extended to all of the back roads of Muncie. If a vehicle stops for SCP-1337, it vanishes from view, to reappear on another road. Should a vehicle pass SCP-1337 by, it will then appear inside the vehicle, where it will re-enact the methods of its murder upon the driver of the vehicle. SCP-1337 will only appear for vehicles containing one person. This escalation matches those events observed following containment attempts of SCP-973. See internal research paper "Apparition Escalation: Preliminary Research into Human Triggered Escalation of Apparition Hostility" by Tamlin et al. for more details regarding this and other similar phenomena.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!
What an accomplishment for an SCP with an interesting history. Good job on the +200, Bright : )

item.title: Staff Post - Closed
item.content:encoded: If repeating the same behavior was a loop, it'd be a very popular tag. In the future, ask before you apply.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Uh… SpecialAgentCoulter, maybe next time, ask about the tag and get a response before adding the tag yourself? I'll message some of the people in charge of that. In the future, if you're not intent on waiting, message Crayne about tags.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could this also be tagged as a "loop?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has gotten blasted in the comments. Personally, I LOVE this. Well written, the "cliche" aspects really made it better for me - (come on guys, in this canon the supernatural is real, where do you think we get our cliches from?) - and all over, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't quite understand the "humanoid apparition level 2"/"humanoid apparition level 5" stuff, or your choice to use "wraith" being that there other more clinical terms to refer to ghosts. But the main problem I have with this is

Dr. L██████, acting without permission, had the E-class Agents executed, and Site 1337-Delta destroyed

Really? I mean, come on, at this point I'm starting to believe that the Foundation hand picks immature assholes from bully recovery farms, gives them some credentials and calls them "doctors". The "Researcher doing something stupid because of stupid and ruining everything" is kind of overdone already. And this is even WORSE because he wasn't punished severely for his actions (I may be the only one, but I believe that what he done was so serious, so unforgivable, that being demoted to D class would be really fitting). Don't get me wrong, this is really entertaining and well written, but you could have easily pulled it off by blaming the killings and the destruction of the site on some administrative mistake or some bureaucratic snafu and not by pointing out one researcher's full fledged idiocy, which at this point seems like beating on a dead horse.

The scip is well loved, and my vote won't probably change anything, but I can't upvote this, nor leave it as a no-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: fool its truly old man jenkins

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Enh… I like to think Decomissioned means they were trying to destroy it, neutralized means it stopped it self, or just happened to stop working… like the difference between being murdered and dying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They aren't actually Foundation Operatives. They're E-class, people who are used because they have previous experience with a specific SCP. I like to think he was sent back for a very SPECIFIC purpose… someone felt he might still be useful, to draw out 1337. But, that's just me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think "Decommissioned" in object class is an error. It should be "Neutralized".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Having had this pointed out to me, I had to change my upvote to a downvote. I mean, shit, the Foundation continues to employ people who they know are narcissistic and stupid enough to disobey clear regulations and actually, literally murder other Foundation employees? Here's all the things he did wrong:

1) He failed entirely to get the approval of, or even notify, anyone else on the project before taking drastic action.
2) He did so with the explicit goal of getting a promotion and bettering himself - the man is clearly self-centred and acts without due care and attention to the Foundation's interests as a whole.
3) He unilaterally decided to attempt to neutralise a Safe-Class object.
4) He murdered two people.
5) He murdered two Foundation operatives.
6) He destroyed a Foundation site.

No way this man is going to be allowed to continue working at the Foundation - he's dangerous, delusional and utterly self-centred with no ability to listen to instructions - not the sort of person you want working on anything anomalous.

I chewed out a draft a week or two ago for pretty much exactly this problem, so I'd be a hypocrite not to downvote here. I can't wrap my head around why the Foundation would allow something like this to go mostly unpunished.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is honestly my biggest problem with it, and my main reason for downvoting (as opposed to neutral-voting). I'm not opposed to the "decommissioned" twist, but I think the way it's done, as well as the results of that (a doctor executing agents and destroying a site, then being allowed to keep working there? What?) kill it for me. Hell, I'd settle for this guy being merely fired and given amnestics, as that's less of a suspension-of-disbelief killer than him being allowed to keep working there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this.

+1 for the first real use of E class that, I think, works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dr. L██████, acting without permission, had the E-class Agents executed, and Site 1337-Delta destroyed

Dr. L██████ was demoted to Junior Staff for his actions, but not reassigned.

An examination of Dr. L██████s journals revealed that he believed decommissioning SCP-1337 would lead to a promotion, and 'recognition of his brilliance.'

How the fuck was this psychopath not arrested and put in prison, much less allowed to stay on board and continue working there for another 10 years?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Special agents were employed to pick up and transport SCP-1337, as no means of moving it were found.

Wait, should that be, "as there are no other means of moving it." or something? Simply, "no means of moving it" should mean they couldn't move it at all right?

All in all, I like this one solely for the fact that it goes from strange to murderous because someone decided to try to decommission it. You really shouldn't do that sort of thing as you're always going to risk making things worse for everyone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
wraith

That alone is worthy of a downvote to me. Is that really something that a scientist would write, someone inherently based in skepticism and rationality?
A failed article a few months ago described its subject as looking like a 'goblin'. I fail to see how this is any better than that.

This also seems to be a pretty standard, if not outright cliche, old ghost story. At the least, it's built primarily of the big ol' bricks of one. Tossing the acronym 'SCP' on top does not change that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is absolutely brilliant, and I don't know whether I should thank or curse you for getting to it before a million crappy SCP's related in some way to 1337 skills were posted

item.title: Re: 100
item.content:encoded: A thousand words, as they say.
From -8 to plus 100. What a difference a picture makes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is how I read this article:

+ show block
– hide block

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Minor nitpick, but shouldn't it be "neutralized" instead of "decommissioned"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Although the Foundation as a whole advocates the protection of non d-class life, individual researchers may vary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not what the Foundation as an organisation does or or advocates, but like any other organisation, we have people who think they know better. In this case that led to SCP-1337 becoming Euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Execution of the Class-E's? That sounds like something the GOC or Chaos Insurgency might do

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Another article whose excellent and gripping story can make me forgive its sometimes egregious tonal problems. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is how you create new problems for yourself, kids.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this does an excellent job of subverting expectations and translating a real life event into an SCP format. There's a pretty good story being told through the documentation, and a little bit of that illuminating the Foundation through the documentation that I enjoy. The formatting kind of looks like ass, though, and is really a sticking point for me. All dat containment strikethrough, yeesh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was going to downvote at first because the inspiration for this entry is so apparent. Then I actually read it through.

It's well done. The separate identification of SCP-1337, 1337-Alpha, 1337-Beta, 1337-Delta, and 1337-Gamma seemed a little excessive, especially since most of them were only mentioned once or twice and just saying "SCP-1337's home" instead of "1337-Delta" or the like is easier for the reader to parse. But that's a common practice that doesn't seem to bother anyone else, so I should probably not care.

It's also another reminder of why the Foundation prefers to contain rather than neutralize anomalies. You never know when you'll make things worse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What bug me is the use of the non-metric measurements in her description. And maybe just me, but "Level 2 Humanoid Apparition, location bound, non-violent, corporeal" read like something from RPG sourcebook about ghost character.

But everything else is great. This is truly a sad SCP story and very scary at same time. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Shame. I was hpoing SCP-1337 would be a -J. And if you don't know what I mean by that, get off the internet this instant.

Otherwise, good SCP. I've heard of the Phantom Hitchhiker story, so when I was this, I thought to nyself, "Hey, wait a minute…. I know this…". +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's an SCP that reads much more like a Tale (is "wraith" a technical term?), but that's not to say I don't like it. No vote for now, but I'll reread it a few times and see if I still like it.

EDIT: Okay, fine, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this article, but a couple things. First, I agree that "decommissioned" should probably be changed to "neutralized". Second, and much more importantly, this article feels unfinished. I don't think the story isn't fully told here. I know an SCP isn't a Foundation Tale, but some SCPs have a narrative to them. SCP-342 is a good example of this. It shouldn't be permanently neutralized, but this deserves a more concrete "ending".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Side note, I still upvoted an earlier draft of this with a different image, but I think the current photo adds a little something to it that was missing before. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For some reason, I never saw this during the original contest except when it was an empty page.

This is a really good SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the progression in this. You start with a ghost story, insert a warning about destroying SCPs, then get a BETTER (scarier) ghost story out of it. Upvoted!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Enh. Its kind of nebulous, what the real point of decom versus neutralised is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Having checked the Object Classes page it says that 'Neutralised' is the correct term for objects which have been destroyed or rendered mundane, while Decommissioned 'is reserved for for objects which have not only been destroyed, but disavowed by the Foundation'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Out of universe, yes. In universe, no.

The disparity is just because it's generally not worth it to write about SCPs that can't do anything any more because they've been melted down to slag.
